# **ASI-302: Galassia-5 Software–Hardware Integration Framework** **Author:** Bryan Kuan

# ![][image1]

- [16. References](#16-references)

## **Abstract**

This project developed and validated a software-hardware integration framework for the Galassia-5 satellite payload subsystem. The work focused on connecting the camera payload, onboard processing hardware, SpaceWire communication, AI-based image inference, and Ku-band radio control into a single end-to-end pipeline. The integration effort centred on the ZSOM-F01 payload computer, which combines a Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC with processing system and programmable logic, together with the practical bring-up needed to make that platform usable for development and testing.

The project began with SpaceWire simulation in Vivado to establish a communication backbone between a camera node and a payload computer node. In the final phase, the work expanded into actual subsystem integration, including support for both the SpaceWire camera path and the Ethernet-based Atlas Lucid / Skunkworks camera path, Hailo-8L enablement, and a more usable Ubuntu-based development filesystem on the ZSOM-F01 board. The final system demonstrates a flexible payload integration framework that can support camera data, AI processing, and Ku-band communication through a common software-hardware architecture.

![][image2]

Figure 1: Mission overview / payload integration with camera, ku band radio and the Hailo AI accelerator with the payload computer

## **1\. Introduction**

Galassia-5 is a satellite project that integrates multiple independently developed subsystems into a single payload architecture. For a spacecraft payload, the key question is not whether each subsystem can function in isolation, but whether the camera, onboard processing, and communication systems can operate together reliably under timing and hardware constraints on the actual mission platform. The purpose of this project was to address that integration challenge.

The project initially focused on establishing a communication backbone. The interim stage developed the SpaceWire architecture, justified its selection, and validated it through a dual-node simulation representing the camera node and the payload computer node. This was necessary because one of the candidate payload cameras (Siemera) communicates exclusively through SpaceWire, requiring the payload computer to support this interface before any meaningful integration could take place.

In the final phase, the scope expanded into a full software–hardware integration framework. This included board bring-up, operating system configuration, camera integration, Ethernet communication, SpaceWire testing, Hailo-based inference, and pipeline timing validation. A major part of the work involved making the ZSOM-F01 payload computer usable as a development platform. The original PetaLinux environment imposed limitations on package management and software installation, while the migration to an Ubuntu-based filesystem enabled a more practical development workflow with access to Python libraries, drivers, and SDK support.

A key complexity in this project is that the final flight camera was not fixed early in the development process. As a result, the integration work had to support two different camera interfaces. The Siemera camera uses SpaceWire and represents the space-grade communication path, while the Skunkworks camera uses Ethernet and requires SDK-based control through Python. Supporting both interfaces ensures that the payload computer can handle different camera configurations and remain adaptable to final mission decisions.

In parallel, the Hailo AI accelerator was integrated and stabilised on the target hardware, as it is essential for meeting the onboard processing time requirements. The final outcome is therefore not a single-interface solution, but a flexible integration framework that supports multiple camera paths, onboard AI processing, and communication subsystems within a unified architecture.

## **2\. Project Problem and Value Proposition**

The central problem addressed by this project was the absence of a unified integration layer for the Galassia-5 payload subsystems. Camera payloads, AI processing, and Ku-band radio were developed as separate components, and without a shared communication backbone they would only be able to exchange information late in the mission development process. That is a serious problem in a satellite project because interface failures are easier to detect and fix while the system is still in development.

The value proposition of the project is therefore twofold. First, it reduces technical risk by making subsystem communication visible and testable early. Second, it produces a payload pipeline that can be validated against the actual mission timing requirement. A system that is accurate but too slow is not mission-ready, and a system that is fast but cannot be integrated reliably is also not mission-ready. The project therefore had to solve both integration correctness and performance.

The project is also valuable because it is reusable. SpaceWire was chosen because it is a real spacecraft protocol with deterministic high-speed links, packetised communication, and full-duplex operation, and because it maps cleanly onto the Zynq UltraScale+ architecture. That decision was technically sound in the interim phase and remains sound in the final system. The later integration work with camera drivers, Hailo, and Ku-band control shows that the communication backbone is not an abstract design exercise, but a platform that can support actual payload work. 

## **3\. Scope of My Work**

My scope was subsystem communication and software integration. In practical terms, that meant designing and validating the communication backbone that connects the camera payload, the onboard AI module, and the Ku-band radio to the ZSOM-F01 payload computer. The interim report already defined that role as the implementation and validation of the link-layer and register-level logic required to make the subsystems talk to each other, together with the testbenches, simulations, and later hardware tests needed for verification.

In the final semester, my role expanded from protocol-level reasoning into a broader integration task. The work included getting the ZSOM-F01 board into a usable operating-system configuration, integrating camera access over Ethernet, bringing up the camera SDK and Python environment, validating Hailo inference on the board, and confirming that the pipeline could process images within mission timing limits. The project scope therefore moved from “communication backbone only” to “communication backbone plus working payload pipeline.”

## **4\. Interim Foundation and Early Validation**

The interim report established several important foundations. It clearly defined the mission problem, namely that the major subsystems were developed separately and had no shared communication layer. It justified the choice of SpaceWire by linking the camera’s interface requirement to the mission architecture. It explained the Zynq UltraScale+ PS–PL split and how a SpaceWire core in programmable logic could be controlled from the processing system using AXI-Lite registers. It also described the dual-node Vivado testbench, the test messages used for link verification, and the simulation-based testing strategy.

That foundation was valuable because it made the early project technically coherent. The project was not just trying a protocol. It had a structured concept: the camera node would be simulated as one SpaceWire endpoint, the payload computer as another, and the PS–PL architecture would later provide software control and subsystem coordination. The interim report also established a staged roadmap for the project, moving from simulation to AXI-Lite control, then to hardware bring-up, and eventually to AI and Ku-band payload integration.

The final semester work can therefore be understood as the completion of that roadmap. The simulation proof-of-concept remained useful, but the emphasis shifted toward making the whole payload environment functional on real hardware, with camera access, AI acceleration, and stable boot and networking configuration.

## **5\. Requirements and Design Constraints**

The project was shaped by functional requirements, timing constraints, and hardware constraints. At the functional level, the system had to support camera acquisition, AI inference, SpaceWire communication, Ku-band control, and structured output generation. At the performance level, the pipeline had to complete within 20 seconds per image. At the platform level, the system had to run on the ZSOM-F01 board, which is the mission-representative payload computer.

The 20-second limit is an important constraint because it defines whether the pipeline is mission-suitable. A pipeline that can produce accurate results but takes too long is not acceptable for a payload that must operate on-board and move data through the system in a controlled way. This is why the Hailo timing work matters so much: it turns the design from “works in principle” into “works within mission budget.”

The project also had to account for interface diversity. The camera path is not identical to the AI path, and the Ku-band radio does not use the same data semantics as the camera. That means the payload computer cannot simply pass bytes around in a generic way. It needs a structured communication and control layer that maps each subsystem into the same overall software architecture without forcing them to share identical hardware protocols.

The hardware constraints were equally important. The final flight camera was not fixed early enough to simplify the integration strategy. The original camera path was SpaceWire-only, but the later work with Skunkworks required Ethernet and SDK-based communication. The board environment also had boot and driver limitations, which made a robust development filesystem necessary. These constraints did not weaken the project; they defined the engineering space in which the integration had to succeed.

![][image3]

Figure 2: Requirements and constraints of the mission. 

## **6\. System Architecture**

The architecture is centred on the ZSOM-F01 payload computer, which acts as the hub for camera input, model execution, hardware communication, and output generation. The underlying chip architecture is a Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC, combining a processing system and programmable logic. This matters because some parts of the design are software-heavy, while others are hardware-heavy, and the PS–PL split allows each side to do the work best suited to it.

The communication backbone originally described in the interim report remains the correct way to think about the system. The PL hosts the low-level, time-sensitive communication logic, while the PS coordinates higher-level software control and payload management. That design is sound because SpaceWire and similar serial payload interfaces benefit from deterministic hardware implementation, while subsystem coordination, logging, and pipeline control are easier to manage in software.

The image-processing pipeline sits above that backbone. It receives an image from the camera, saves it to storage, splits it into tiles, runs Hailo YOLOv8 inference, detects objects such as ships, converts detections into coordinates, and produces output. The tiling step is not arbitrary. It is required because the deployed HEF model expects 800×800 input, while the camera image may be much larger. Tiling preserves detail and allows the model to process the scene in manageable sections.

This architecture is modular, and that is one of its strongest features. The camera path can change, the AI accelerator can be substituted or updated, and the radio integration can be expanded without rewriting the whole system. That is important for a payload integration framework because mission hardware is often not fully fixed at the beginning of the project.

![][image4]

Figure 3: Overall Block Diagram of the payload Computer ZSOM-F01

## **7\. Camera Integration**

The camera work in this project is notable because it covers two different communication paths. The original interim focus was the Siemera camera, which only supports SpaceWire. That made SpaceWire the obvious first protocol to validate, and the interim report showed how the dual-node simulation could verify bidirectional communication between a camera node and a payload computer node.

The final semester broadened this work to include the Atlas Lucid / Skunkworks camera, which uses Ethernet. That shifted the integration problem from protocol simulation to practical networking and software control. The board has two Ethernet ports, but only `eth0` could be used reliably for the camera connection. This meant that the camera and the ZSOM-F01 board had to be placed on the same network using DHCP before communication could succeed. In other words, the camera problem was not solved by plugging in a cable; it required proper interface selection, network alignment, and driver/software readiness.

To make the camera controllable from the board, the Lucid Atlas SDK was installed and configured. Python was then used as the control layer, including the SDK import.

![][image5]Figure 4: Image of simple test using python to communicate with the Skunkworks camera using the SDK configured

That is a meaningful integration step because it turns the camera into a programmable subsystem rather than a standalone device. Once Python can talk to the camera SDK, the camera becomes part of the payload’s software workflow and can be included in pipeline testing, file capture, and inference preparation. This also demonstrates a key integration principle: the camera interface is not just electrical, it is software-defined.

![][image6]Figure 5: Process of setting up SDK on payload computer to communicate with Skunkworks camera

The two camera paths together strengthen the report. The SpaceWire path shows that the architecture can support a space-grade interface. The Ethernet path shows that the software stack can also support a practical camera SDK and networking setup. That makes the final system more flexible and more representative of the mission uncertainty around final camera selection.

![][image7]Figure 6: ifconfig of eth0 successfully configured to communicate with the Skunkworks camera in the same network of 10.42.0.0/24

![][image8]Figure 7: A test done using Python programming to retrieve information of the Skunkworks camera through Ethernet connection

## **8\. Ku-band Radio Integration**

Ku-band radio was not physically present in the lab during this phase, so full hardware testing could not be completed directly. Instead, the SpaceWire-based control path was validated using a loopback test between the ZSOM-F01 payload computer and an external computer. Since the Ku-band radio uses the same SpaceWire protocol, this loopback test provides a realistic verification of the communication layer that the radio would use once the hardware arrives.

This matters because it means the integration work is still meaningful even without the physical radio. The loopback test confirms that the SpaceWire driver path works, that the payload computer can exchange data correctly over the expected protocol, and that the communication backbone is ready for the actual Ku-band unit when it becomes available. The radio itself was not tested in the lab, but the protocol path was validated and should transfer to the real radio hardware.

Ku-band integration matters architecturally because it validates the idea of a shared communication backbone. If the camera, AI module, and radio can all be coordinated through the same overall software structure, then the payload system is easier to maintain and easier to extend. That is particularly valuable for a satellite mission because it reduces the cost of future subsystem additions and avoids one-off integration logic.

![][image9]Figure 8: SpaceWire loopback test for Ku-band path

The SpaceWire loopback test is used to validate the communication path between the payload computer and external subsystems that rely on the SpaceWire protocol, specifically the Ku-band radio and the Siemera camera. Since both of these subsystems use SpaceWire as their primary interface, confirming correct operation at the protocol and driver level is essential before full hardware integration.

The diagram illustrates the complete data flow across four main layers: user space, kernel space, programmable logic (FPGA), and the physical SpaceWire interface. At the user level, a Python application interacts with the SpaceWire driver through a wrapper (spw.py). This wrapper handles data transmission and reception in chunks, ensuring that data written to the interface is correctly returned and verified. The verification step is important, as it checks that transmitted data matches received data, confirming end-to-end integrity.

In the kernel layer, the SpaceWire driver (/dev/sw0) manages communication between software and hardware. It provides standard file operations such as read, write, open, and release. When data is written from the Python application, it is passed into transmit (TX) buffers and prepared for hardware transfer. Similarly, received (RX) data is retrieved from hardware buffers and passed back to user space.

The programmable logic layer is responsible for high-speed data movement and protocol handling. The AXI DMA controller plays a key role by transferring data between system memory (DDR) and the SpaceWire core without heavy CPU involvement. The transmit path (MM2S) streams data from memory to the SpaceWire interface, while the receive path (S2MM) captures incoming data and stores it back into memory. The SpaceWire bridge implements the protocol encoding, link control, and packet handling required for proper communication.

At the hardware level, the SpaceWire PHY handles the physical signalling. In this test, a loopback configuration is used, where transmitted data is directly routed back to the receiver. This eliminates the need for external hardware while still validating the complete transmission path.

The importance of this test lies in its ability to verify the full communication stack. Even though the Ku-band radio is not physically available, the successful loopback test demonstrates that the SpaceWire driver, DMA pipeline, and protocol implementation are functioning correctly. As a result, it provides strong confidence that both the Ku-band radio and the Siemera camera will operate correctly when connected, since they rely on the same validated SpaceWire interface.

## **9\. Software Environment and Board Bring-Up**

One of the most important parts of the final work was getting the ZSOM-F01 board into a usable software environment. The board originally booted with PetaLinux, which is reasonable for embedded systems but inconvenient for the kind of integration work needed in this project. The limitations were practical and recurring: internet access was difficult, package management was cumbersome, and the command-line environment was not pleasant for iterative development and debugging.

The board was therefore migrated to an Ubuntu-based root filesystem. That decision was not trivial, because it required understanding the boot chain and filesystem layout. The process involved applying the patch, fixing the `system-user.dtsi` file, rebuilding the image, and generating the boot files. The SD card was then split into a boot partition and a root partition. The boot partition was populated with the PetaLinux-generated boot files, while the root partition was replaced with the Ubuntu filesystem.

There was a practical complication: the files on the SD card could not just be copied casually because root permissions were required. That was solved by using the appropriate privileged file-copy process. Another necessary detail was preserving the `modules` and `firmware` directories from PetaLinux. Without those pieces, the board would not boot and the environment would not behave correctly. A new boot configuration also had to be added so that U-Boot would mount and boot the Ubuntu filesystem rather than the default PetaLinux root filesystem. In effect, the boot chain became: bootloader, then U-Boot, then kernel, then the Ubuntu root partition.

This environment change made the rest of the project much easier. Once Ubuntu was running, it became possible to connect to the internet, download libraries, work with Python packages, and use the drivers needed for camera and Hailo integration. That made the entire project more productive and more scalable. 

![][image10]

![][image11]

![][image12]Figure 9:  Boot chain / Ubuntu filesystem

![][image13]Figure 10: Ubuntu successfully booted up in payload computer

## **10\. Hailo Integration and AI Enablement**

The Hailo work is one of the most important integration tasks in the project because the AI accelerator was initially difficult to bring up. In the earlier payload-computer work, the Hailo 8L accelerator was physically connected, but the software could not detect it using `lspci`. That meant the system was not yet configured correctly for hardware acceleration, and the AI path remained blocked even though the board had the necessary hardware.

The eventual solution came from research into the correct software and platform versions. The project needed Ubuntu 22.04 specifically, and the ZES-recommended stack allowed PetaLinux 2024.2 together with HailoRT 4.23, which supports the Hailo model used in the project. Once that configuration was aligned properly, `lspci` could detect the Hailo 8L accelerator. That was a critical milestone because it proved that the hardware was not the problem; the issue had been software compatibility and environment selection.

![][image14]Figure 11: Hailo successfully detected by the payload computer

That discovery also explains why the Ubuntu filesystem became so useful. PetaLinux is lightweight, but it made the system harder to use for driver installation, internet access, package management, and debugging. Ubuntu, by contrast, made it easier to install and use the software needed for camera and Hailo integration. In a project like this, the practical ability to install and run libraries matters as much as the nominal weight of the operating system. The result is that the system became much more useful once Ubuntu was booted and the necessary libraries, drivers, and Python support were available.

The Hailo accelerator is central to the performance story because CPU-only inference is too slow for the mission requirement. With Hailo active, the pipeline can run in roughly 4.3 to 4.5 seconds per image, which is far below the 20-second limit. That shows that hardware acceleration is not just a performance improvement; it is a mission requirement enabler.

![][image15]Figure 12: example of a pipeline testing done 

## **11\. Ship Detection Pipeline and Proof-of-Function**

The ship-detection work was developed in two stages. The first stage was to make the ship-detection code function correctly on a single image or tile set, so that Hailo inference could detect and label ships reliably. The second stage was to extend that working detection logic into a complete benchmarked pipeline that measures conversion, tiling, inference, geolocation, and duplicate removal separately. This progression matters because it shows the project moving from a functional object-detection prototype to a verified payload workflow.

The underlying ship-detection code performs the following operations: it converts GeoTIFF satellite imagery to JPG when required, divides the image into overlapping tiles, runs YOLOv8 inference on each tile using the Hailo accelerator, converts tile detections into geographic coordinates using the image centre, satellite state vector, and ground sampling distance, and finally removes duplicate detections caused by overlap before producing the final CSV output. A separate inference script is used to validate the object-detection stage itself. That script loads the tiled images, runs Hailo inference, visualises the detections, reconstructs the full image, translates tile labels back into the original image space, and crops the detected ships for inspection.

The ship-detection pipeline therefore has two purposes. First, it demonstrates that the onboard AI module can detect ships and generate labelled outputs. Second, it provides a structured data product in CSV form that can later be passed to the Ku-band radio path for downlink. In the final payload architecture, the pipeline is the data-processing step that connects camera input to mission output.

### **Five-stage pipeline**

**Stage 1 — Image Conversion**  
GeoTIFF imagery is converted into JPG format for processing. This step ensures the input is compatible with the computer-vision pipeline and the Hailo-based inference flow.

**Stage 2 — Image Tiling**  
The large satellite image is split into overlapping tiles so that no ship is lost at the border of a tile. The final configuration uses 800×800 tiles because that matches the compiled Hailo model. The overlap is needed so the same target can still be detected when it lies near a tile boundary.

**Stage 3 — AI Inference (Hailo Accelerator)**  
Each tile is processed by the YOLOv8 ship-detection model compiled as a Hailo HEF file and executed on the Hailo-8 accelerator. For every ship found, the model returns a bounding box and a confidence score.

**Stage 4 — Geolocation (Pixel → GPS)**  
The pipeline converts detections from pixel coordinates into real-world latitude and longitude using the image centre latitude/longitude, the satellite state vector, and the ground sampling distance.

**Stage 5 — Duplicate Removal**  
Because tiles overlap, the same ship can appear in more than one tile. The pipeline removes duplicate detections by checking targets near tile borders and keeping the higher-confidence result.

The final outputs of the pipeline are the reconstructed detections, the translated labels, the cropped ship images, and the results CSV. These outputs demonstrate the full chain from raw image to final labelled result.

![][image16]

Figure 13: Ship detection output

![][image17]  
Figure 14: CSV output of detected ships

## **12\. Experimental Methodology and Timing Evaluation**

The timing evaluation was conducted using a structured benchmarking approach designed to analyse both system performance and hardware behaviour under realistic operating conditions. The benchmark framework separates the pipeline into component stages so that the major runtime costs can be identified clearly. In particular, the split timing measurements isolate tiling and AI inference, while the overall benchmark modes compare single image processing and batch image processing. This allows the report to distinguish between one-time model loading overhead and steady-state per-image processing time.

The results show that the full pipeline comfortably satisfies the mission requirement of less than 20 seconds per image. More importantly, the timing breakdown shows that the pipeline is not a black box: the dominant cost comes from image preparation and inference, rather than from the post-processing stages. In the ZES measurements, tiling and inference together take the majority of the runtime, with the single image processing case showing approximately 2.34 seconds for tiling and 2.71 seconds for inference, while the batch processing case shows approximately 2.28 seconds for tiling and 2.08 seconds for inference. These values indicate that the accelerator is functioning correctly and that the pipeline cost is driven mainly by the amount of image data that must be processed.

The split benchmark is particularly useful because it shows the effect of reusing the model. The difference between single image processing and batch processing operation confirms that model initialisation adds overhead, but only a relatively small amount compared with the total pipeline cost. This means the system should be operated in a batch processing mode whenever multiple images are processed, because that avoids repeated setup cost and gives a more representative runtime for continuous mission operation.

These results support the use of the Hailo accelerator. CPU-only processing exceeded the mission constraint in earlier testing, reaching approximately 22 seconds per image in batch processing mode and up to 42 seconds per image in single image processing, while also suffering from memory limitations. That makes CPU-only execution unsuitable for the mission pipeline. Hailo is therefore not an optional upgrade; it is a necessary part of the design because it brings the inference stage into a practical runtime range.

The Raspberry Pi 5 comparison must be interpreted carefully. The Hailo benchmark on Raspberry Pi 5 shows faster raw timings for the tiling and inference stages, with approximately 0.31 seconds for tiling and 0.78 seconds / 0.65 seconds for inference in single image processing and batch processing respectively. That does not automatically make Raspberry Pi 5 the better final payload computer, because the report is not evaluating raw speed alone. The evaluation is based on whether the board can support the full mission architecture. The ZSOM-F01 board remains the correct final platform because it is the mission-representative payload computer and supports the FPGA/SpaceWire integration path required by the overall Galassia-5 design.

The purpose of these tests was to validate both functional correctness of subsystem integration and compliance with the mission timing constraint.

![][image18]

Figure 15: ZES board component timing comparison showing tiling and inference time in single image processing and batch processing modes.

![][image19]  
Figure 16: Raspberry Pi 5 Hailo timing comparison showing faster isolated tile and inference timings, but without changing the final payload-board selection.

## **13\. Hailo versus CPU, and ZES versus Raspberry Pi 5**

CPU-only inference is too slow for the mission pipeline and does not provide a practical route to real-time processing. In the earlier baseline testing, CPU-only execution exceeded the mission limit and also ran into memory pressure, making it unsuitable for the final payload configuration. Hailo is therefore the correct inference choice because it makes the end-to-end pipeline technically viable.

The platform comparison must be presented carefully. If Raspberry Pi 5 appears faster in a raw benchmark, that result only shows that it is a strong general-purpose computing platform. It does not make it the best payload computer for Galassia-5. The ZES board remains the correct final platform because it is the mission-representative computer, it supports the FPGA-based integration path needed for SpaceWire, and it matches the actual payload architecture far better than a general-purpose development SBC. ZES boards are also designed specifically for space environments using proprietary technologies that protect commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) components, whereas the Raspberry Pi 5 is a consumer product that lacks this protection, making it highly susceptible to radiation-induced failures. In other words, Raspberry Pi 5 may be faster for this isolated benchmark but the ZES board remains the only choice for the payload computer.

The correct decision criteria are therefore not only speed, but also mission compatibility, subsystem integration, and hardware relevance. In other words, Raspberry Pi 5 may be useful as a comparison platform, but ZES is the correct final integration target because it is the board that can support the full communication backbone, the camera path, the AI module, and the Ku-band integration together.

![][image20]Figure 17: Comparison between ZES payload computer and the Raspberry Pi 5

## **14\. Conclusion**

This project successfully developed a software-hardware integration framework for the Galassia-5 satellite payload subsystem. The work began with a SpaceWire communication backbone and a dual-node simulation environment, and it ended with a working integration stack that includes camera access, AI inference, Ku-band control, and a usable development environment on the ZSOM-F01 payload computer. The project therefore combines protocol design, embedded bring-up, software integration, and performance evaluation into one coherent final system.

The strongest technical result is the pipeline timing. The measured image-processing time is comfortably below the 20-second mission requirement. The strongest design result is the hardware choice: Hailo is required because CPU-only processing is too slow and too memory constrained, and ZSOM-F01 is the correct platform because it is the real mission payload computer. The strongest integration result is the ability to access the camera through Ethernet and Python, while still preserving the SpaceWire-based architecture needed for the original mission concept.

The final report therefore presented as evidence that Galassia-5 has a viable, tested, and mission-relevant payload integration framework. It does not merely describe components. It explains how those components were made to work together, why the key decisions were made, and how the final system satisfies the project’s technical goals. 

## **15\. Future Work**

Although the system meets the current project objectives, several areas can be improved. First, the Ku-band radio should be integrated and tested physically once the hardware becomes available to validate end-to-end communication. Second, the image tiling stage can be optimised, as it is currently the main contributor to runtime. Third, the system can be further enhanced by improving robustness for real mission conditions, including error handling and long-duration operation.

Future work may also include migrating from the Ubuntu-based development environment to a lighter deployment system for space operation, as well as further optimisation of the AI pipeline for power and thermal efficiency.

## **16\. References**

[1] ECSS, “SpaceWire – Links, nodes, routers and networks,” ECSS-E-ST-50-12C, 2008.

[2] Xilinx, “Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC Overview,” AMD Xilinx Documentation, 2023.

[3] Hailo, “Hailo-8 AI Processor Datasheet,” Hailo Technologies, 2023.

[4] Ultralytics, “YOLOv8 Documentation,” 2024. Available: https://docs.ultralytics.com

[5] NASA, “Remote Sensing and Ground Sampling Distance (GSD),” NASA Earth Observatory.

[6] Wertz, J. R., Everett, D. F., & Puschell, J. J., *Space Mission Engineering: The New SMAD*, Microcosm Press, 2011.
